axioms.name: prakasaka
axioms.cite: the nimitta that releases each — the crushing of this sandal, the burning of that resin

axioms.name: niyata
axioms.cite: is niyata and pratidravyam avasthita, fixed and lodged substance by substance

axioms.name: asraya
axioms.cite: fixed and lodged substance by substance

axioms.name: prakasaka
axioms.cite: So likewise similar words each have their own dedicated sound-patterns

denials.name: constitutes
denials.cite: the dedication is no argument that the pattern constitutes the word

claims.name: prakasaka
claims.cite: the illuminating condition even of things similarly apprehended — smells and the rest

rejected_readings.label: manifestable_as_nimitta
rejected_readings.rendering: The manifestables themselves—smells—are the illuminating condition for similar things perceived.
rejected_readings.why: Confuses the manifestable (gandha, sort=manifestation) with the manifestor (nimitta, sort=power). The commentary explicitly identifies the nimitta as the crushing or burning, not the smell itself. This sort error makes the prakāśaka relation unlicensed.

rejected_readings.label: nimitta_universal
rejected_readings.rendering: The same instrumental cause (crushing) produces the scent in all sandals indifferently.
rejected_readings.why: Asserts that nimitta is universal/non-specific, which contradicts the pratidravyavasthita property: it must be lodged substance by substance. The axioms require asraya (ground) to each dravya individually.

rejected_readings.label: pattern_constitutes_word
rejected_readings.rendering: The dedicated sound-pattern for each word is what constitutes that word.
rejected_readings.why: The commentary explicitly rejects this: 'the dedication is no argument that the pattern constitutes the word.' The relation is manifestation (prakāśaka), not constitution or composition. The word is not made of the sound.

notes: The verse exemplifies the manifestation-relation via analogy: just as similar smells have dedicated, fixed, substance-specific nimittas (crushings, burnings), similar words have dedicated sound-patterns. This normalizes the word-sound relation, rendering it non-mysterious within everyday perception. The core claim is asymmetrical (nimitta is power, gandha is manifestation), and the denial of constitution is key to the doctrine: relation does not entail composition.
